share.share_text:
1. He stops initiating.
He still shows up when invited. But he hasnât planned anything himself in months. Nothing feels worth the effort of starting.

2. Heâs always tired but sleep doesnât fix it.
He wakes up exhausted and goes to bed the same way. This isnât a body problem. Heâs tired in a way that rest canât reach.

3. He stops talking about the future.
Ask him about next year and he gives vague answers. Not because heâs uncertain. Because he canât picture anything feeling different from right now.

4. Heâs irritable over small things.
He snaps at people who donât deserve it. Gets frustrated over nothing. Itâs not stress. Itâs a man carrying something heavy with nowhere to put it down.

5. Heâs there but not really there.
His body is present. His mind is somewhere else entirely. He nods at the right moments but hasnât actually heard anything in a long time.

6. He stopped doing the things he used to love.
He didnât decide to quit. He just slowly stopped showing up. And didnât notice until weeks had passed.

7. He deflects every real conversation.
Ask him how heâs doing and he says fine. Heâs not hiding something. He genuinely doesnât know how to answer anymore.

8. He numbs himself at night.
Scrolling. Watching anything. Not because heâs enjoying it. Because sitting with himself feels worse.

9. He looks completely fine from the outside.
Still working. Still showing up. Still functioning. Thatâs what makes this so easy to miss.
A man who has lost his joy doesnât look broken. He looks normal. Just quietly empty inside.

Most men donât call it losing joy.

They call it being tired. Being busy. Needing a holiday.

But a man can have a full life, a good income, people around him and still feel nothing.

If this is someone you know, check on him. Not with âyou okay?â with time, presence, and patience.
